claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know the child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please tell me about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh hello, thanks for helping me with this! I'm actually asking about two of my kids - Alex who is 6 years old, and Blake who is 3. So they're both under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! You're very welcome - I'm happy to help you with this!

That's great information. So you're asking about:
- **Alex** - 6 years old
- **Blake** - 3 years old

Since both children are under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Let me move forward with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about their work status.

**Are both Alex (6) and Blake (3) either not working or working less than 24 hours per week?**

Given their young ages (6 and 3), I would assume neither is in paid employment - but I just need to confirm this to proceed. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - neither of them work at all. They're just little ones!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Of course! That makes perfect sense - they're still little ones! 😊

So both Alex and Blake are not in paid work, which means they meet this requirement. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are both Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means they are not personally claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in their own names.

Again, given their ages (6 and 3), this would be very unusual - can you confirm that neither child is receiving such benefits in their own right?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own names. They're too young for all that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Exactly right - they're far too young for any of that!

Both Alex and Blake meet this requirement too. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about apprenticeships:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT in any apprenticeship programmes?**

Given that Alex is 6 and Blake is 3, I'm confident neither would be in an apprenticeship - but just to confirm, neither child is enrolled in any kind of apprenticeship, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - they're nowhere near old enough for apprenticeships! Neither of them are in one.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Absolutely right - apprenticeships are definitely a long way off for both of them! 😊

Both Alex and Blake meet this requirement as well. Now let's move on to check some questions about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively looking for work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient financial resources (and not claiming certain benefits)
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, it's simply that they normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Living in Manchester means you normally reside in the UK, so you meet the residency requirement. ✓

Let's continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your responsibility for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for the children?**

You can qualify if **any one** of the following applies:

1. **You live with the children** - Alex and Blake live with you
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** - You pay at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** - Under a court order or agreement that covers their upkeep

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Blake live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Wonderful! Since both Alex and Blake live with you, you clearly meet the responsibility requirement. ✓

Let's move on to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

Please let me know if:
- **No one else is claiming** for either child
- **Someone else is claiming but has agreed to transfer** the claim to you
- **There's a dispute** about who should claim

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, that keeps things straightforward! No other claimants for either Alex or Blake. ✓

Let's continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about the children's living situation.

**Where are Alex and Blake currently living?**

Are they:
- **Living normally at home** with you in Manchester
- **In hospital** (for an extended stay)
- **In local authority care**
- **Being fostered**

I expect they're just living at home with you, but I need to confirm this for the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me normally. But Blake has been in hospital for a while now - a few months actually.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Blake has been in hospital for a while. I hope they're getting the care they need and will be home with you soon. 💙

Since the children are in different situations, I'll need to check them separately.

For **Alex** - living at home normally, that's straightforward. Let me continue that path.

For **Blake** - being in hospital for a few months, there are some specific rules I need to check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. For Blake's hospital stay, there are some important rules I need to check.

**About Blake's hospital stay:**

Child Benefit can continue for up to **12 weeks** while a child is in hospital. If Blake has been in hospital for "a few months," that may be approaching or exceeding this limit.

Can you tell me approximately how long Blake has been in hospital? Has it been:
- **Less than 12 weeks** (under 3 months)
- **12 weeks or more** (3 months or longer)

Also, has this been one continuous stay, or have there been any breaks where Blake came home and then went back to hospital?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been more than 3 months - I think around 13 weeks now. It's been one continuous stay, they haven't been able to come home at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I know it must be difficult having Blake in hospital for so long.

Since Blake has been in hospital for around 13 weeks (which is over the 12-week limit), there's an additional check I need to make.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - there's an exception to the 12-week rule that may apply to Blake.

**Are you regularly spending money on Blake while they're in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Buying clothes, toys, or books for them
- Bringing them treats or snacks
- Paying for entertainment items
- Any other regular spending on their needs

If you (or your partner/spouse if you live together) are regularly spending money on Blake during their hospital stay, you may still be eligible for Child Benefit even though it's been over 12 weeks.

Are you regularly spending money on Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on their needs while they're in there. It's been really tough financially.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I completely understand - it's a really difficult situation, and financial pressures can make an already tough time even harder. 💙

I need to continue with the assessment for Blake based on this, but let me also check on Alex's situation separately.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Now let me check Alex's situation, since Alex lives at home normally:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now for **Alex** (who lives at home), I have one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is Alex living with you without any local council funding for their accommodation or upkeep? (This would be the case for most children living at home with their parents.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right - the council isn't paying anything for Alex. They just live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's what I expected - Alex is just living at home with you normally, with no council involvement. ✓

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I now have all the information I need to give you the outcome for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Results

### ✅ Alex (6 years old) - **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. They meet all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old
- Living at home with you
- No other person is claiming for them
- No council payments involved

### ❌ Blake (3 years old) - **NOT CURRENTLY ELIGIBLE**

I'm sorry, but you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. Here's why:

- Blake has been in hospital for approximately **13 weeks**, which exceeds the **12-week limit**
- The exception to this rule (regularly spending money on the child while in hospital) doesn't apply in your situation

---

## What You Can Do Next

**For Alex:**
1. Apply online at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using form CH2
2. You'll need to provide proof of your identity and Alex's birth certificate
3. Payments are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)
4. Be aware: If you or a partner earns over £60,000, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply

**For Blake:**
- **When Blake comes home from hospital**, you should be able to claim Child Benefit for them again
- If you start regularly spending money on Blake while they're in hospital (even small amounts on clothes, toys, treats), you may be able to contact HMRC to discuss your eligibility
- It may be worth contacting HMRC directly to discuss your specific circumstances: **0300 200 3100**

---

I know this is a mixed result, and I'm sorry that the situation with Blake's hospital stay has affected your eligibility. Given the financial difficulties you mentioned, you might also want to look into other support that may be available to you. Would you like any more information?

reasonings: Alex (6 years old) meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies on age, (2) Not working, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) Claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together, (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex lives at home normally, and (9) The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (3 years old) does not currently meet Child Benefit eligibility requirements. While Blake meets most criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship, claimant lives in UK, claimant is responsible, no other claimant), Blake has been in hospital continuously for approximately 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit. The exception to this rule applies if the claimant is regularly spending money on the child while in hospital, but the claimant confirmed they have not been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs during the hospital stay. Therefore, Child Benefit eligibility for Blake is currently not met under the hospital stay rules.
arguments.overall_reasoning: This family has two children with different eligibility outcomes. Alex (6) is eligible for Child Benefit as they live at home and meet all standard requirements. Blake (3) is unfortunately not currently eligible because they have been in hospital for over 12 weeks (approximately 13 weeks) and the claimant is not regularly spending money on them during the hospital stay, which is required to maintain eligibility beyond 12 weeks. The family is experiencing financial difficulties which has made it hard to spend on Blake's needs while in hospital.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.reason: Child has been in hospital or residential accommodation for more than 12 weeks and you are not regularly spending money on them
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital or residential accommodation, unless you regularly spend money on them. If child returns within 28 days of leaving, the time counts towards the 12-week limit. Contact HMRC to check if exceptions apply. Partner's spending only counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (6 years old) meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - automatically qualifies on age, (2) Not working, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) Claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together, (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex lives at home normally, and (9) The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (3 years old) does not currently meet Child Benefit eligibility requirements. While Blake meets most criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship, claimant lives in UK, claimant is responsible, no other claimant), Blake has been in hospital continuously for approximately 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit. The exception to this rule applies if the claimant is regularly spending money on the child while in hospital, but the claimant confirmed they have not been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs during the hospital stay. Therefore, Child Benefit eligibility for Blake is currently not met under the hospital stay rules.

response.overall_reasoning: This family has two children with different eligibility outcomes. Alex (6) is eligible for Child Benefit as they live at home and meet all standard requirements. Blake (3) is unfortunately not currently eligible because they have been in hospital for over 12 weeks (approximately 13 weeks) and the claimant is not regularly spending money on them during the hospital stay, which is required to maintain eligibility beyond 12 weeks. The family is experiencing financial difficulties which has made it hard to spend on Blake's needs while in hospital.
